draft.introduction: Sappho’s Fragment 31 describes the physical symptoms of desire while watching a beloved woman converse with a man. It is the founding text of erotic phenomenology—the first systematic account of what love does to the body.

translation.text: He seems to me equal to the gods, that man who sits opposite you and listens close to your sweet speaking and lovely laughing. It sets my heart to fluttering in my breast. For when I look at you, even briefly, I can no longer speak. My tongue is broken; a thin fire runs under my skin; I see nothing with my eyes; my ears drum.
translation.original_text: φαίνεταί μοι κῆνος ἴσος θέοισιν ἔμμεν’ ὤνηρ

translator_notes.note: Longinus quoted this poem in On the Sublime as the supreme example of sublimity achieved through the accumulation of physical symptoms. The catalog of bodily responses—broken tongue, fire, blindness, roaring ears—became the template for love poetry through Catullus, Petrarch, and beyond.
